critic_raw:
The design uses a stratified random split on 100,000 patient admissions 'assuming temporal autocorrelation is sufficiently weak.' As with other readmission designs in this set, this assumption is contradicted by the RNN's own architectural rationale: if RNNs with attention mechanisms are better because they capture 'sequential interactions within patient data,' then temporal autocorrelation is by hypothesis meaningful, not negligible. The split strategy is inconsistent with the model's theoretical motivation.

The design lists recall as the primary metric 'to identify as many true readmissions as possible.' Recall alone without a paired precision constraint is not a useful clinical metric — a model that predicts all patients as high-risk achieves perfect recall at zero precision. The design does not specify a minimum precision threshold or a target operating point, making the metric optimizable in a degenerate way. Furthermore, recall as the sole optimization criterion during model selection on the validation set will drive the model to predict positive for essentially all patients, which is clinically useless.

The design states demographic and hospital identifiers are 'within the feature set' to 'mitigate the risk of confounds.' Including hospital identifiers as features means the model learns hospital-specific readmission base rates, which is a form of using group membership to adjust predictions. This is potentially a fairness concern (different hospitals may have different patient demographics) and operationally questionable — if the model is deployed at a new hospital not represented in training, hospital ID provides no signal. More critically, if hospital ID is a strong predictor due to systematic differences in documentation or coding practices rather than genuine medical differences, the model will exploit this shortcut rather than learning transferable clinical patterns.

The design evaluates 100,000 admissions from 'the past three years.' This is a hospital system spanning three years during which COVID-19 substantially changed admission patterns, discharge timing, and readmission definitions. A random split mixes pandemic and non-pandemic admissions in both train and test, hiding the distribution shift that any deployed model would face when moving from one temporal regime to another.

The baseline is logistic regression 'optimized through grid search.' No regularization path, feature count, or constraint on model complexity is mentioned. An unconstrained logistic regression on clinical and demographic features from 100,000 admissions can fit hundreds of features and will not represent the simple, clinically interpretable model that serves as the practical comparator in real hospital settings. The baseline needs to be constrained to a clinically interpretable specification (e.g., LACE index features) to be a meaningful comparator.
all_issues_raised: The stratified random split assumes weak temporal autocorrelation while the RNN's architecture is motivated by strong temporal dependencies — these assumptions directly contradict each other within the same design.

Recall as the sole primary metric without a paired precision constraint or operating point specification is optimizable by predicting all patients as high-risk, which is clinically useless; no target sensitivity or precision floor is defined.

Including hospital identifiers as model features encodes hospital-specific base rates as a shortcut: the model learns systematic coding or documentation differences rather than transferable clinical patterns, and will fail to generalize to new hospitals.

The 3-year dataset spans COVID-19, which substantially changed admission patterns and readmission definitions; a random split hides this temporal distribution shift and will produce optimistic generalization estimates.

The logistic regression baseline is unconstrained by feature count or clinical interpretability requirements; an unconstrained grid-searched logistic model on hundreds of EHR features is not the clinically relevant comparator (e.g., LACE index or similar parsimony-constrained models) and inflates the apparent complexity of the baseline.
all_issues_adjudicated: The stratified random split assumes weak temporal autocorrelation while the RNN's architecture is motivated by strong temporal dependencies — these assumptions directly contradict each other within the same design.

Including hospital identifiers as model features encodes hospital-specific base rates as a shortcut: the model learns systematic coding or documentation differences rather than transferable clinical patterns, and will fail to generalize to new hospitals.

The 3-year dataset spans COVID-19, which substantially changed admission patterns and readmission definitions; a random split hides this temporal distribution shift and will produce optimistic generalization estimates.
